2025 · arXiv · Benchmarks & Evals

PRBench: Large-Scale Expert Rubrics for Evaluating High-Stakes Professional Reasoning

Scale AI built a 1,100-task, 19,356-criterion benchmark of real legal and finance work — authored by 182 credentialed professionals across 114 countries — and found that frontier models top out at just 0.39 (Finance) and 0.37 (Legal) on its hardest problems.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Afra Feyza Akyürek, Advait Gosai, Chen Bo Calvin Zhang, … Bing Liu, Yunzhong He · Scale AI
venue arXiv citations 16 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-06-18
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Frontier-model progress is tracked almost entirely through academic benchmarks with verifiable answers — GPQA, ARC-AGI, MMLU, AIME, Humanity's Last Exam — all math, science, and coding. They are clean to grade and they saturate fast. But they say little about whether a model is any good at the messy, open-ended work people actually pay experts to do.

That gap is concrete. Usage analyses from Anthropic's claude.ai and OpenAI's chatgpt.com both put Legal and Business & Financial tasks among the most common professional uses of chat models — and among the highest-stakes, where a wrong answer costs money, exposes liability, or destroys trust. Yet these domains are barely covered by systematic evaluation.

The usual fallback for open-ended quality — preference-ranking arenas like Chatbot Arena, AlpacaEval, Showdown, and Arena Expert — gives a coarse aggregate vote that is noisy, subjective, and ungrounded in expertise. The few rubric-based professional benchmarks that exist are either private (BigLaw Bench, CorpFin) or tiny (ProfBench at 80 tasks, APEX at 200), so they can't support reproducible, fine-grained measurement. PRBench is built to be the opposite: large, public, expert-authored, and scored against detailed rubrics.

// how it works

The method

PRBench is 1,100 expert-authored tasks — 600 Finance, 500 Law — each paired with an expert-curated rubric. Across both domains it ships 19,356 criteria (10,264 Finance, 9,092 Legal), which the authors call the largest public rubric-based benchmark for either field. Every task was written by one of 182 qualified professionals: Legal contributors hold a JD or international equivalent; Finance contributors hold a Master's, a CFA, or have 6+ years of experience. They were asked to write the questions they (or their clients) actually face — analysis and judgment, not exam-style recall — yielding tasks that span 114 countries and 47 US jurisdictions across 13 Finance and 12 Legal topics.

Roughly 30% of tasks are multi-turn (up to 10 turns). User turns are written by the human experts; intermediate assistant turns are sampled from open models (GPT OSS 20B, Mistral Medium, DeepSeek R1) to build realistic context — and the rubric grades only the final model response. Following HealthBench's recipe, the authors rank tasks by average model difficulty and carve out a Hard subset of 300 Finance and 250 Legal tasks for frontier models. The 1,100 public tasks are released; a private heldout set is retained to watch for future contamination.

Weighted, signed rubrics

Each criterion gets an integer weight from −10 to +10 (never 0). Positive criteria describe desired qualities ("cites the controlling case"); negative criteria flag undesired ones ("invokes the automobile exception when it doesn't apply"). Annotators first pick one of six severity levels — from Critically Important [+8,+10] down to Critically Detrimental [−10,−8] — before assigning the number, which forces hierarchical reasoning and reduces weight noise. Rubrics must be constructive, mutually exclusive yet collectively exhaustive, atomic, objective (binary true/false), and self-contained.

An LLM judge grades each criterion independently as satisfied (1) or not (0). A response's score is the weighted credit it earns, divided by the total positive weight available; the model's overall score is the mean across tasks, floored at zero:

sj = Σi wj,i · Ij,iΣi: wj,i>0 wj,i     S(M) = max ( 0, 1n Σj sj ) // Ij,i ∈ {0,1} is the judge's verdict on criterion i; negative criteria can only subtract, and the per-task score caps below at 0

Choosing the judge

To pick a grader, the authors had three candidate judges (o4-mini, GPT-4.1, Claude Haiku 4.5) and two human experts each grade GPT-5 and Claude Sonnet 4.5 responses over 101 tasks. Agreement with experts was on par with expert-vs-expert agreement: o4-mini reached Cohen's κ 0.603 / Macro-F1 0.801 and GPT-4.1 0.605 / 0.802, against an expert-expert baseline of κ 0.589 / F1 0.813 (Claude Haiku 4.5 lagged at 0.535). They chose o4-mini for its lower cost. For per-category comparison they also define a min-normalized score that subtracts each task's minimum possible score, so categories whose criteria are mostly negative (e.g. Supplemental Insight) stay comparable.

// what they found

The results

Across 20 leading models — each evaluated three times with 95% confidence intervals — there is a lot of headroom. On the full set the best scores are 0.51 (Finance) and 0.50 (Legal); on the Hard subsets they fall to 0.39 (Finance) and 0.37 (Legal). GPT-5 and GPT-5 Pro consistently lead, followed by Grok 4 Fast Reasoning, with open-weight Kimi K2 Thinking and GPT OSS 120B close behind the proprietary frontier. Newer models score consistently higher, so professional reasoning is improving — just slowly, and from a low base.

Tools barely help. The tasks are designed to be solvable by reasoning alone; enabling web search improves O3 and Grok (e.g. O3 on Legal rises from 0.352 to 0.398) but hurts most others through over-reliance on external sources (GPT-5 on Finance slips from 0.394 to 0.381), and a code interpreter adds nothing beyond search. Every tool-enabled Hard-subset score stays ≤ 0.4.

The category breakdown is where the benchmark earns its keep: Instruction Following is nearly solved (~0.65–0.70 for every top model) while Supplemental Insight and Handling Uncertainty lag (often ~0.22–0.34). GPT-5 leads Practical Utility, Handling Uncertainty, and both Accuracy categories — and beats Grok-4 by more than 10 points on Legal Procedural Correctness — while Grok 4 Fast Reasoning dominates Legal Risk & Ethical Disclosure. Gemini 2.5 Pro punches above its overall rank on Instruction Following.

That divergence gets stark at the cluster level: on the Legal cluster "advanced corporate and international tax law," GPT-5 scores 0.64 while Grok-4 manages only 0.16; on a comparable Finance tax-strategy cluster, Claude Opus 4.1 lags at 0.34 against a top score of 0.76. Length is not the lever — GPT-5 and O3 sit on the efficiency frontier, while GPT-OSS 120B, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude Sonnet 4.5 write nearly twice as much for similar or worse scores. The recurring failure modes are inaccurate judgments, missing process transparency, and incomplete reasoning — reliability gaps, not raw-knowledge gaps.

SubsetBest FinanceBest Legal
Full set (1,100 tasks)0.510.50
Hard subset (300 / 250)0.390.37
Hard + tools (≤, O3 leads)0.400.40
// does it hold up?

The eval read

The baselines are about as strong and current as a late-2025 paper can field — GPT-5 and GPT-5 Pro, o3 and o4-mini, Grok 4, Claude Opus 4.1 and Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash, Kimi K2 Thinking, GPT OSS — each run three times with confidence intervals. The expert grounding is real: 182 credentialed authors, a two-expert authoring-plus-validation pipeline, and a 93.9% independent agreement rate on rubric validity. This is a serious benchmark, not a leaderboard stunt.

Two things deserve scrutiny. First, the headline metric. A score of 0.39 is the fraction of positive-weight credit captured, with the per-task value floored at zero — so a response that commits a Critically-Detrimental −10 error can still post a respectable number if it also hits many small positives, and the floor means catastrophic answers and merely-mediocre ones can collapse to the same 0. In high-stakes law and finance, the question that matters is "did it avoid the disqualifying error?", and a weighted-mean-of-many-criteria doesn't report that; there's no separate critical-criterion pass rate. Second, the judge: o4-mini was chosen for cost, its κ ≈ 0.60 is only "moderate" agreement, and it was calibrated on just 101 tasks grading two model families — one of which (GPT-5) shares lineage with the judge, leaving room for self-preference that the κ study doesn't isolate.

On leakage, the design is thoughtful but time-limited: tasks are freshly expert-authored (little present-day contamination), yet the 1,100 public tasks will inevitably seep into training corpora, so the public score has a shelf life — the retained private heldout is the instrument to actually trust over time, and the paper would be stronger if it committed to publishing the public-vs-heldout gap as models update.

baselines

20 current frontier models (GPT-5/Pro, o3, Grok 4, Claude Opus 4.1/Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 2.5, Kimi K2, GPT OSS); 3 runs each with 95% CIs — strong and current.

contamination

1,100 tasks released (public score will erode); freshly authored to limit present-day leakage; a private heldout is retained but its gap-over-time isn't yet reported.

what's missed

The weighted-rubric mean (floored at 0) rewards breadth and can mask a single disqualifying error; no separate critical-criterion pass rate; open-ended rubrics encode one expert's view where professionals reasonably disagree.

would raise confidence

Report worst-case / critical-criterion behavior, diversify judges beyond the GPT family, add an end-to-end "would a professional trust this?" human study, and track the heldout-vs-public gap.

// bottom line

PRBench is a credible, large-scale, expert-grounded measure of professional reasoning where it has economic teeth, and its headline — frontier models cap at ~0.39/0.37 on the Hard subsets — is believable and useful. The caveat is that a clipped weighted-rubric mean is an aggregate that can hide whether a model avoids catastrophic, high-stakes errors, and the eval's longevity rests on an o4-mini judge of moderate agreement plus a public set that will degrade. Watch the private heldout.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Frontier models cap at 0.39 (Finance) and 0.37 (Legal) on PRBench's Hard subsets — large headroom in real professional reasoning even as academic benchmarks saturate.
  2. It's the largest public rubric benchmark for law and finance: 1,100 expert-authored tasks and 19,356 weighted criteria from 182 credentialed professionals (JD / CFA / 6+ years), spanning 114 countries and 47 US jurisdictions.
  3. Models with similar overall scores diverge sharply by capability (GPT-5 0.64 vs Grok-4 0.16 on advanced tax law); Instruction Following is near-solved while Supplemental Insight and Handling Uncertainty lag, and verbosity doesn't buy quality.
  4. The recurring failures are inaccurate judgments, missing process transparency, and incomplete reasoning — reliability gaps, not knowledge gaps — which is exactly what blocks professional adoption.
